Tafseer:
The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was informed of the jinn's reaction through revelation:
The words 'Say, It has been revealed' (Qul uhiya) make it entirely clear that the reactions of the jinn described in this Surah were not communicated to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) directly by the jinn, but through divine revelation. A group of them, while on their way, heard the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) reciting the Qur'an. Its attraction so captivated their hearts that they became entirely absorbed in listening to it, and were then so affected by this pleasing speech that when they returned, they arose to call their nation to it.
The purpose of narrating the incident: What occasion is this referring to? It is difficult to give a definitive answer. I believe this is the detailed account of the incident mentioned briefly in Surah Al-Ahqaf. We clarified its context and background there in the light of narrations. You were informed of these reactions of the jinn so that you might report them to your nation: while your treatment of this eloquent speech is that you put your fingers in your ears upon hearing it and rise as enemies against the one who communicates it—even though this speech was revealed for your benefit—the competent jinn, upon hearing it, become so enamored of it that they rise to call their nation to it, even though they are not its direct addressees.
The word 'Qul' (Say) is a proof that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was informed of these reactions of the jinn so that you might communicate them to the leaders of the Quraysh, in order to evoke some self-respect in them. This also contains comfort for you, ensuring you do not feel disheartened by the disregard of the unworthy. If these people do not appreciate it, the fault lies neither in this speech nor in you, but is the result of the corruption of their own hearts.
The call of the jinn to their nation: 'And they said, "Indeed, we have heard an amazing Qur'an"' (fa-qalu inna sami'na qur'anan 'ajaba). This is the call they delivered to their people after listening to this speech. That is, upon hearing it, they did not merely appreciate it verbally, but regarded it as an essential requirement of appreciating the truth and well-wishing for their nation to share with them the heavenly blessing Allah Almighty had bestowed upon them.
'Ajab' is a verbal noun (masdar); therefore, in contrast to 'ajeeb', it contains an intensive meaning. They used this word regarding the beauty, impact, and wisdom of this speech. In Arabic, this word is used not only to express the unique nature of something, but to highlight its pleasing and impactful aspect. Here, it is used in this very sense. In Surah Al-Ahqaf, this same point is described in these words:
(They said, 'O our people, indeed we have heard a Book revealed after Moses confirming what was before it which guides to the truth and to a straight path.') (Al-Ahqaf, 46:30).
This also reveals that the jinn understand not only their own language, but also the language of the humans in the region where they reside, and among them are those who are capable of appreciating the beauty and flaws of that language.

The Qur'an's call to guidance:
This explains the pleasing nature of the Qur'an to which the word 'amazing' points. That is: this Book guides to the truth and guidance that every sound nature accepts. In the aforementioned verse of Surah Al-Ahqaf, the words 'which guides to the truth and to a straight path' appeared. Here, the comprehensive word 'rushd' (guidance) is used for the same subject. This word encompasses all the fundamental beliefs and virtues that are endowed within human nature. Unless a person ruins their nature through evil deeds, this Book guides them in the correct direction, and even if a veil occasionally covers them due to negligence, it is easily removed through minor reminders, provided the person does not devalue the truth in pursuit of desires. The highest status in this 'rushd' belongs to monotheism. It is the source of all fundamental beliefs and deeds, and their soundness depends on it.
The right of guidance: 'So we believed in it' (fa-amanna bih) - This states the natural right of this guidance: when Allah Almighty informed us of this guidance, we regarded it as a natural duty to believe in it; hence, we accepted it with a sincere heart.
The source of guidance: 'And we will never associate with our Lord anyone' (wa lan-nushrika bi-rabbina ahada) - The source of all guidance, as noted, is monotheism; therefore, they described the requirement of believing in this guidance as: 'It is no longer possible for us to associate anyone with our Lord.'
It should noted here that all fundamental beliefs and virtues are shared not only between humans and jinn, but the Qur'an clarifies that they are shared throughout the universe. The difference between us and the jinn lies in social laws. There is no reason for any difference in monotheism, Resurrection, recompense, virtues, and vices. Consequently, these very matters are mentioned here, and during the initial phase of the Meccan period, the Qur'an invited people to these fundamental demands of human nature.

This further explains their statement: 'And we will never associate with our Lord anyone'—showing that: 'It has become entirely clear to us that the majesty of our Lord is completely pure and elevated above the relationships of wives and children. Foolish are those who attribute such things to Him. He has neither taken a wife nor a son.'
'Jadd' means majesty, status, and rank. That is: His self is so elevated that nothing can be His partner, associate, or equal. He is completely unique, self-sufficient, and independent of everything. No one can be His equal.
Although this is the statement of the jinn, and therefore should have been under 'And they said, "Indeed, we have heard"', since the Prophet was informed through revelation, it was placed under 'Say, It has been revealed to me that...', and all subsequent statements of the jinn will appear under it.

Instruction to avoid following foolish leaders:
'Safeeh' means foolish. Here, the jinn used this word for their leader because, after listening to the Qur'an, his foolishness became clear to them.
'Shatat' refers to a statement that is extremely distant from truth and justice.
When the reality of monotheism became clear to the jinn, they informed their nation: 'Our foolish leader has been fabricating these statements—which are extremely distant from the truth—against Allah, claiming He has a wife and children, and that such and such are His sons and daughters. But the Qur'an we heard has made it clear that these statements of our foolish leader were completely baseless. Thus, we have repented of these myths, and we invite our nation to repent as well and not fall into the trap of this foolish leader.'
This indicates that these jinn belonged to the class of common folk. They had been following the path their leaders directed, but when the reality became clear to them, they threw off the yoke of their leaders' obedience with complete courage and walked on the straight path established by Allah. This was communicated to the common people of the Quraysh through the Prophet so that they too might find the courage to emerge from the trap of their foolish leaders and rely on their own intellect and insight.

Warning against a delusion:
This points to the delusion because of which they remained trapped in the path of their leaders. They said: 'We assumed that humans and jinn could never dare to fabricate such lies against Allah, claiming He has associated partners and made them worthy of worship. But they did dare, and we fell into their trap due to our simplicity.'
This is their warning to the common people of their nation: 'Do not lock your intellect in the name of the sanctity of these priests, but use your own understanding. Let it not be that they hand you a snake in the name of a fish.'

Pointing to some foolish practices of humans:
In this connection, they also shared their discovery with their nation: 'Through this Qur'an, we also learned that some men among humans used to seek refuge in some men among the jinn, but instead of bringing any benefit, this only increased them in distress and misery.'
'Rahaq' literally means imposing a burden on someone beyond their capacity. From this, its usage extended to excess, sin, injustice, and transgression. Thus, further on in verse 13, this word is used in the sense of transgression.
Generally, commentators have interpreted the verse to mean that some foolish humans, by seeking refuge in the jinn, made the jinn highly arrogant. However, this interpretation does not seem correct. First, the benefit of such a statement is not clear; second, it exceeds the root meaning of 'rahaq'. In my view, the subject of 'zadu' (they increased) is 'rijalan minal-jinn' (men among the jinn), and the reference of 'hum' (them) is 'rijalum-minal-ins' (men among humans)—meaning: foolish humans sought refuge in the jinn to protect themselves from their evils, but the jinn, seeing that some humans had fallen into their net, made them dance to their tune instead of protecting them.
Among the polytheists of the Arabs, there was a delusion that the jinn are the source of knowing the unseen; this very delusion established the system of soothsaying among them, which was built entirely on falsehood and deceit, as clarified in its relevant place. The soothsayer would threaten those trapped in his net, saying: 'A highly dangerous jinni is angry with you; unless you sacrifice such and such or offer a certain vow for him, he will bring a calamity upon you,' and the person would inevitably execute his command. Indeed, under the command of these soothsayers, some foolish individuals would even sacrifice their children to please the jinn, as mentioned in Surah Al-An'am.
Another delusion was that every valley and mountain is inhabited by a specific group of jinn. If one had to spend the night in that valley, it was necessary to seek the refuge of the leader of the jinn of that valley; otherwise, he might bring a calamity. Thus, during the pre-Islamic period, when the Arabs camped in a valley, they would seek the refuge of its leader jinni according to their assumption. This was obviously another unnecessary trouble in which the delusion of the jinn had trapped them.
These are the practices to which these believing jinn pointed, and their objective is to show how foolish humans and wicked jinn colluded due to a lack of monotheism, producing various spiritual and physical corruptions, for the eradication of which Allah Almighty revealed this Book.
The indefinite form of 'rijal' (men) can point to both contempt and significance. You can interpret it to mean: some foolish humans sought refuge in some foolish jinn; or: some wicked humans sought refuge in some wicked jinn; or even: some foolish humans sought refuge in some wicked jinn—all of which are grammatically correct.

Reference to the Resurrection after monotheism:
Following monotheism, this points to the misunderstanding of both groups regarding the Resurrection—showing that: 'Just as you fell into the misunderstanding that Allah would never resurrect anyone after death, humans likewise fell into this misunderstanding.' The meaning is: this Qur'an has been revealed to remove this misunderstanding of the people as well.
Some have interpreted this to mean: 'Just as there was a misunderstanding among you that Allah would never send another messenger, humans likewise had this misunderstanding. The revelation of this Qur'an removed this misunderstanding as well.'
Although the wording can accommodate this meaning, it is contested by the fact that the primary addressees of the Qur'an, whether the Children of Ishmael or the Children of Israel, were awaiting the appearance of a messenger based on past prophecies. Although the People of the Book tried to cover them up, they could not deny them. Among the Children of Ishmael, due to their unlettered state, the concept of this messenger was not very clear, but they did not deny it completely. Indeed, in some places of the Qur'an, they are rebuked, reminding them: 'Before the appearance of the Messenger, you used to claim that if Allah sent a messenger among you, you would be the first to accept his call and follow his guidance; but when Allah blessed you with this favor, you rose to oppose him.'

Reference to a new experience:
This refers to a specific experience they had in the system of the universe for the first time during the period of this Book's revelation. They informed their nation: 'During this period, we examined the heaven and found that the lowest heaven has been filled with strict guards and shooting stars, and the places where we used to sit to overhear the secrets of the high assembly are no longer accessible. If anyone tries to do so now, he will find a shooting star waiting for him.'
It is stated in various places in the Qur'an that when the devils of the jinn try to overhear the secrets of the unseen, they are pelted with shooting stars. This personal experience of the jinn confirms this, and furthermore, reveals that while there was some room for overhearing previously, during the period of this experience, every corner of the heaven was filled with guards and shooting stars, leaving no room for the jinn.
While the jinn mentioned this major change in the universe, they could not explain its cause to their people, because—as appears from the subsequent verse—the wisdom of this change was not entirely clear to them. However, combining this experience with the revelation of the Qur'an, they conveyed the impression that this change in the heaven was related to the revelation of the Qur'an.
In our view, their قیاس (deduction) was correct. Various places of the Qur'an clarify that the guard established in the heaven to protect it from the reach of the devils was made extremely strict during the period of revelation, so that they could find no way to interfere with the revelation. While this guard has always existed—and shooting stars were pelted at the devils even before the revelation of the Qur'an—just as governments block every path of a highway along which the royal treasury is being transported or the king's procession is passing, similarly, during the period of revelation and the coming and going of Gabriel, the heaven was blocked from every side to close every path for the thieves.
This report of the jinn is mentioned here to show the polytheists of the Arabs: 'Your accusation that this is the speech of soothsayers, which a jinni casts upon the Prophet, is completely baseless. The statement of the jinn themselves is sufficient to refute it, showing that during this period, obtaining any news from the heaven was impossible for them.'
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 8 and 9 was combined in the original text.]

Pointing to a blessed conjunction for the inhabitants of the earth:
Since the actual cause of this major change in the universe was not entirely clear to the jinn, they did not state any definitive cause but merely expressed their uncertainty: 'While its cause is not clear to us, it is indeed a precursor of a major change on the earth. Whether this change will be a source of evil for the inhabitants of the earth or contains some great guidance from their Lord, the future will decide.'
Although they did not state their opinion definitively out of caution, the style of the discourse shows that they regarded it as a good omen for the inhabitants of the earth. Thus, they mentioned the evil in the passive voice, but mentioned the expectation of guidance in the active voice: 'or whether their Lord intends for them guidance' (am arada bihim rabbuhum rashada). The difference in the style of these two phrases from the perspective of respect for Allah was clarified under Surah Al-Kahf. What is worth noting here is that if they had not possessed an overwhelming thought that it contained goodness for the inhabitants of the earth, they would have stated this second phrase in the passive voice as well. However, seeing the Qur'an and this change side-by-side, their minds went to the view that both events belonged to the same category, serving as a blessed conjunction.

Association and separation should be based on faith and deeds:
That is: 'Until now, there was no distinction between goodness and evil in our eyes; the righteous and the wicked were equal to us. But this Qur'an has cleared our misunderstanding, making it clear that we are not all on the same path, but our paths and creeds are diverse, and we must keep this difference in view. Our association and separation should be based on faith and disbelief, rather than claiming: "Whoever belongs to our nation and tribe is ours, whether he is a disbeliever or a believer, righteous or wicked."'
This represents their declaration of disassociation (bara'ah) from those of their nation who would persist in shirk and disbelief even after this call to faith. All prophets made a similar declaration before their nations, and the reformers of every age followed this sunnah of the prophets—an example of which is the attitude of the Companions of the Cave described in Surah Al-Kahf.
'Taraiq' means paths and creeds, and 'qidad' means diverse.

Human beings are in God's hand at all times:
Following the call, this is their warning to their nation: 'It has become clear to us that we are in the hand of God's power at all times. He can seize us whenever He wills. We can neither escape Him by hiding in the earth, nor by fleeing into the heavens.'
According to the recognized style of Arabic, some contrasting words are omitted here, which are understood from context. For example, 'in the earth' (fil-ard) was expressed in the first phrase; therefore, 'in the heaven' (fis-sama') was omitted in the second. Similarly, 'by flight' (haraba) appeared in the second phrase; therefore, its contrast was omitted in the first. These omissions have been unpacked in the translation.

Encouraging those who accept faith:
This is their presenting of their own example before their people: 'When this guiding call reached our ears, we regarded it as our duty to appreciate it; hence, we believed in it.' The meaning is: this is the very way that must be adopted by all those who possess respect for truth and guidance. Those who flee from it bear witness that they want to follow their desires instead of intellect.
'And he will not fear deprivation or burden' (fa-la yakhafu bakhsaw-wa-la rahaqa) - 'Bakhs' means decrease, and 'rahaq' means excess/transgression.
This is also expressed as: (And he will not fear injustice or deprivation) (Ta-Ha, 20:112).
This encourages those who accept faith: those who believe should remain assured that this is not a losing transaction. Allah Almighty will recompense their good deeds fully; they will have no fear of any right being decreased, nor of any excess being committed against them. Everyone will face what they have done: (So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it) (Az-Zalzalah, 99:7-8).

The obvious consequence of distinguishing between good and evil:
That is: 'Since Allah Almighty has endowed us with the capacity to distinguish between good and evil, has highlighted both good and evil through His prophets and books, and we are witnessing the reality that some of us are obedient and some are disobedient, He must inevitably treat both of them in accordance with His justice and wisdom—showing that those who choose His obedience will be worthy of Paradise, while those who deviate from the truth will become fuel for Hell.' This distinction is an essential requirement of His justice and wisdom; otherwise, this world is an unjust place where goodness and evil are equal in His sight.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 14 and 15 was combined in the original text.]

The discourse turns directly to the Quraysh:
After referencing the statements of the jinn, the discourse turns directly to the Quraysh. In the first verse, we wrote that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was instructed to communicate these statements of the jinn to the ungrateful and arrogant of the Quraysh. Now, the Quraysh are warned directly: if they remain firm on the straight path, it is not a losing transaction, but the path of success in this world and the Hereafter. We would have poured upon them rain in abundance.
'Ma'un ghadaq' literally means abundant water, but it is an Arabic metaphor for abundance of sustenance and favor. This appeared in the previous Surah:
(Ask forgiveness of your Lord. Indeed, He is ever Forgiving. He will send [rain from] the heaven upon you in abundance, and provide you with increase in wealth and children, and make for you gardens and make for you rivers) (Nuh, 71:10-12).
This clears the delusion of the Quraysh who regarded their prosperity as the favor of their idols, fearing that if they accepted the call of the Qur'an, they would lose their wealth and children. It is stated: it is their foolishness that they have attributed Allah's blessings to their imaginary deities. These blessings belong to Allah, and since He bestowed them despite their ungratefulness, He will preserve and increase them even more if they choose gratitude.
Monotheism is a well-known path: 'on the path' (ala-t-tariqah) refers to the straight path of monotheism. It is described as if it is a well-known path, which is a reality because human nature bears witness to it, intellect points to it, signs in the universe bear witness to it, and Allah's messengers and books have called to it.

A timely warning:
This is a timely warning: the sustenance and favor We bestow in this world are not a proof of one's being approved by Him, but a trial to see whether the recipient remains grateful and obedient, or grows ungrateful and arrogant. The meaning is: the arrogant leaders of the Quraysh forgot this obvious truth. They regarded these blessings as their hereditary right, and in their pride, they turned away from the reminder Allah revealed to show them the path.
'And whoever turns away from the remembrance of his Lord—He will commit him to a climbing punishment' (wa man-yu'rid 'an dhikri rabbihi yasluk-hu 'adhaban sa'ada) - This is another warning: those who turn away from their Lord's reminder in their worldly pride should remember that Allah will commit them to a punishment that will increase constantly.
'Dhikr' (remembrance) refers to the Qur'an, for whose disregard the Quraysh were rebuked. This word is used in various places for the Qur'an.
'Adhaban sa'ada' (climbing punishment) has been interpreted as 'severe punishment', but 'sa'ad' literally means to climb or increase. Therefore, I believe it refers to the reality that the punishment of those who reject the Messenger and His Book is not temporary, but increases constantly. The punishment they face in this world will be followed by a greater punishment in the Hereafter, and their punishment will increase constantly, without any decrease.

Places of worship belong to Allah alone:
While the preceding verse used the third person, this addresses them directly: the mosques are specifically for the worship of Allah, so do not associate anyone in worship with Him in them. This monotheism is part of the warning. That is: when Allah seizes those who turn away, these imaginary deities will not save them; so do not pollute Allah's mosques with their worship.
Since Allah alone is worthy of worship, every mosque by its very purpose of construction belongs to Allah alone. No mosque can be constructed for anyone else, nor can anyone else be worshipped in any mosque.
Although 'masajid' (mosques) is general, the address is to the Quraysh; therefore, context indicates that it refers primarily to the House of Allah (the Kaaba). Describing it in the plural form is because it is the Qiblah and center of all mosques, and also because using the plural makes the command completely general. A similar style appeared in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 17: (It is not for the polytheists to maintain the mosques of Allah). There too, 'the mosques of Allah' refers primarily to the Kaaba, but the plural is used to make the command general.

The most natural thing has become an anomaly:
That is: while the Kaaba should have been a place where no name besides Allah is heard, the situation is completely opposite: when the servant of Allah stands to worship Him alone, the opponents crowd around him from every side.
'Libada' is the plural of 'libdah', which refers to things crowded and packed together. This portrays the situation: while the Kaaba was constructed solely for the worship of Allah alone, monotheism has become such an anomaly in it that when the Messenger of Allah stands to worship his Lord and recites the Surahs of pure monotheism, people crowd around him from every side, regarding him as an anomaly or mad. The same happened when he went out to deliver the call and recite the Surahs of monotheism: the wicked would crowd around him to insult and torment him.
'The servant of Allah' ('abdullah) refers to the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). This word contains affection, and expresses the reality: the most natural and fitting thing for the servant of Allah is to call upon Him alone, yet the conscience of the world has become so corrupted that this very act has become an anomaly and source of dislike for the people.

Decisive declaration of the Prophet:
The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is instructed: no matter how much they object or dislike it, pay no attention to them but inform them clearly: 'I only call upon my Lord, and I will never associate anyone with Him, no matter how great you assume your partners to be.'

It is commanded: tell them: 'If you dislike my call and are not ready to listen to my words, I have no cure for that. I am sent as a caller, not appointed as a guardian over you. Neither your harm and benefit lie in my hand, nor your guidance and deviation. My duty is only to communicate what Allah has commanded, so I am performing this task. Beyond this, I bear no responsibility.' Note the style of grammar here: after 'harm' (darra), 'benefit' (naf'a) and the contrast to 'guidance' (rashada) were omitted, as the contrast itself points to them.

This further explains: 'If I, to please you or out of fear, were to accept anyone as a partner with Allah, I would commit a major fabrication against my Lord, from whose punishment no one would protect me, nor could I find any refuge against Him.'

This verse is related to 'I do not possess for you [power of] harm or right direction'. That is: 'I can neither show you the punishment upon your demand, nor place guidance in your hearts. My task is only to communicate to you the message with which Allah has sent me, and to inform you of His commands.'
Using 'His messages' (risalatih) after 'notification' (balagha) points to the reality: 'My official duty is only to communicate clearly. Beyond this, I have no responsibility regarding your guidance or deviation, nor do I have the authority to make any alteration in God's commands to please you.'
'And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger—then indeed, for him is the Fire of Hell, they will abide therein forever' (wa man-ya'si-llaha wa rasulahu fa-inna lahu nara jahannama khalideena feeha abada) - That is: 'Having performed this duty of communication, I am absolved of responsibility. Those who still persist in disobeying Allah and His Messenger should remember that for them is the Fire of Hell, in which they will abide forever.'

That is, today they boast of their power and population; but when they face the punishment they are threatened of, they will know who is weaker in helpers and fewer in numbers—them, or the Messenger of Allah whose warning they mocked.
'What they are promised' (ma yu'adun) encompasses the punishment that befell the nation of Noah in this world due to their denial, as well as the punishment they will face in the Hereafter. This theme appeared in Surah Nuh, verse 25.

That is, tell those who are hastening the punishment or the Resurrection, asking you when it will occur: 'God sent me only to warn of it, so I have warned you. As for when it will occur, I have no knowledge of it. Perhaps it is near, or my Lord has postponed it for a term.'

That is: the true Knower of the unseen is He alone. He does not disclose the knowledge of the unseen which He has reserved for Himself to anyone. 'On His unseen' (ala ghaybihi) does not refer to every unseen matter, but to the specific unseen He has reserved for Himself—such as the exact time of the punishment and the Resurrection. Allah Almighty does not disclose this knowledge even to His angels and messengers.

As for His messengers whom He chooses for His mission, it is not necessary for Him to disclose all secrets of the unseen to them. Fulfilling the mission of messengership does not require them to be knowers of the unseen; however, He establishes guards before them and behind them to ensure they communicate the message of their Lord.
This exception is not a continuous exception, but is similar to the one in Surah Al-Ghashiyah:
(So remind, [O Muhammad]; you are only a reminder. You are not over them a controller. Except him who turns away and disbelieves—then Allah will punish him with the greatest punishment.) (Al-Ghashiyah, 88:21-24).
This is 'istithna'-i-munqati'' (an exceptional clause that introduces an independent statement clarified by the subsequent clause).

'Ya'lama' (that He may know) is used here in the same sense as: (that We may know those who strove among you) (Muhammad, 47:31) and other similar verses. Similarly, 'linandhura' (that We may see) appeared in Surah Yunus, verse 14. Allah knows everything, but He wills to expose what is within the heart through a test.
'And He has encompassed whatever is with them and has enumerated all things in number' (wa ahata bima ladayhim wa ahsa kulla shay'in 'adada) - That is: He keeps the trust of His Shariah and His revelation, which He delivers to His messengers, under His strict supervision. He keeps count of everything; not the smallest detail can escape His count.
